journal-meta.journal-id: Chin Med J Pulm Crit Care Med
Chin Med J Pulm Crit Care Med

journal-title-group.journal-title: Chinese Medical Journal Pulmonary and Critical Care Medicine

title-group.article-title: Unveiling the role of the respiratory microbiome in long COVID pathogenesis and therapeutics

contrib-group.aff: Key Laboratory of Molecular Biophysics of the Ministry of Education, Hubei Key Laboratory of Bioinformatics and Molecular-Imaging, Center of AI Biology, Department of Bioinformatics and Systems Biology, College of Life Science and Technology, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, Hubei 430074, China

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

body.p: Long COVID, characterized by a constellation of persistent symptoms following acute severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection, represents a growing public health challenge with significant impacts on multiple organ systems, particularly the respiratory tract. Symptoms such as chronic cough, fatigue, and neurocognitive dysfunction often continue for months, impairing quality of life and functional capacity. The respiratory microbiome, comprising dynamic communities of bacteria, viruses, and fungi, is essential for maintaining immune homeostasis and epithelial defense. Its alteration during SARS-CoV-2 infection may underpin many of the prolonged inflammatory and structural changes observed in long COVID patients.1

Emerging evidence highlights the role of microbiome dysbiosis in driving immune dysregulation, impairing mucosal barrier function, and facilitating secondary infections. Additionally, systemic interactions—especially along the gut–lung and gut–brain axes—amplify inflammatory processes and contribute to diverse clinical manifestations. The depletion of beneficial bacteria and reduced production of immunomodulatory metabolites, such as short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs), further exacerbate pulmonary and extrapulmonary sequelae in long COVID patients.2

This article examines the interplay between respiratory microbiome alterations and long COVID, from molecular mechanisms to clinical implications. By addressing current methodological challenges and outlining future research directions, we aim to foster a deeper understanding of the microbiome’s role in long COVID and facilitate the development of targeted, effective treatments.

In healthy individuals, the respiratory tract harbors diverse microbial communities, including commensal genera such as Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, and Corynebacterium. These organisms contribute to host defense by competing with pathogens for nutrients and adhesion sites, producing antimicrobial compounds, and finely modulating local and systemic immune responses. A balanced microbiome is essential for respiratory health, offering resilience against infections and inflammatory diseases.3

SARS-CoV-2 infection causes direct damage to airway epithelial cells, resulting in impaired barrier function and altered local immune activity.4 The virus binds to angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptors highly expressed on respiratory epithelial cells, leading to cell death and reduced secretion of antimicrobial peptides. This damage facilitates the overgrowth of opportunistic pathogens such as Staphylococcus aureus and Klebsiella pneumoniae. Additionally, the robust inflammatory response—characterized by elevated levels of interleukin (IL)-6, IL-1β, and tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α)—further disrupts the ecological balance of microbial communities, promoting dysbiosis that can persist long after the acute infection phase.5

Clinical studies indicate that COVID-19 significantly reduces microbial diversity in the respiratory tract. There is a marked decrease in commensal bacteria such as Streptococcus and Veillonella, whereas opportunistic pathogens like Pseudomonas and Enterobacteriaceae become more abundant. These changes are often correlated with disease severity and can persist for months after the acute infection, potentially contributing to the chronic respiratory symptoms observed in long COVID.6

The persistence of dysbiosis may lead to chronic inflammatory states, impaired tissue repair, and fibrotic changes in the lungs.7 The loss of beneficial bacteria reduces the production of immunomodulatory metabolites, such as short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs), which are crucial for maintaining immune homeostasis. This may result in ongoing bronchial hyperreactivity and elevated risk of secondary infections, forming a vicious cycle of inflammation and microbial imbalance.8

Respiratory microbiome dysbiosis drives persistent immune activation in long COVID by disrupting the balance between pro-inflammatory and regulatory pathways. A marked shift toward Th17 responses amplifies the production of IL-17 and IL-23, promoting neutrophil recruitment and sustained mucosal inflammation. Concurrently, reduced regulatory T-cell (Treg) function diminishes anti-inflammatory signals, allowing unchecked cytokine release. Elevated levels of IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-1β correlate strongly with fatigue, myalgia, and respiratory discomfort, suggesting systemic immune involvement (Fig. 1).9, 10Fig. 1
Microbial-mediated mechanisms of long COVID. Microbiome dysbiosis in long COVID causes Th17/Treg imbalance, immune overactivation, and epithelial barrier damage, increasing pathogen colonization. Biofilm formation and antibiotic resistance sustain infection–inflammation cycles. Reduced metabolites like SCFAs promote chronic inflammation via epigenetic changes, leading to T-cell exhaustion and lung fibrosis. Gut–brain axis disruption also contributes to neuroinflammation and neurological symptoms. LPS: Lipopolysaccharide; SCFA: Short-chain fatty acid; Th17: T helper 17 cell; Treg: Regulatory T cell.Fig 1

Long-term immune alterations may also involve trained immunity and epigenetic modifications in innate immune cells, potentially driven by persistent microbial dysbiosis and inflammation. For instance, persistent antigenic exposure or molecular mimicry between viral and microbial antigens could not only exacerbate autoinflammatory conditions, but also entrain innate immune cells toward a hyperresponsive state. Furthermore, microbiome-derived metabolites such as SCFAs are often reduced, weakening their inhibitory effects on histone deacetylase (HDAC) and nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells (NF-κB) pathways (Fig. 2). This complex interplay between dysbiosis and host immunity underscores the need for immunomodulatory therapies targeting both microbial and inflammatory components. Dysbiosis compromises respiratory epithelial integrity by down-regulating tight junction proteins (e.g., claudin-1 and occludin) and mucin genes (e.g., MUC5AC), leading to barrier leakage and increased permeability. Impaired mucociliary clearance results in mucus accumulation, creating niches for pathogenic bacteria such as Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus to form biofilms. These biofilms protect bacteria from antibiotics and immune attacks, perpetuating chronic infection and local inflammation.11 The resulting chronic bronchial inflammation and recurrent respiratory infections contribute to long COVID symptoms such as persistent cough and sputum production. In severe cases, prolonged epithelial injury may lead to remodeling and fibrotic changes, further diminishing lung function. Therapeutic strategies aiming to restore epithelial health—such as probiotics, barrier-enhancing agents, or mucolytics—may help break this cycle of inflammation and tissue damage.Fig. 2
Mechanisms by which gut microbes influence respiratory health. SCFAs, produced by gut flora from dietary fiber, enter the lungs via blood circulation. There, they activate GPCRs on macrophages and epithelial cells, suppress NF-κB, lower pro-inflammatory cytokines (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α), and enhance Treg differentiation. Separately, gut commensals activate ILC2s, which migrate to the lungs via S1P signaling, promoting type 2 immunity (e.g., eosinophil recruitment) and worsening allergic asthma. ATP: Adenosine triphosphate; FoxP3: Forkhead box protein P3; GPCRs: G protein-coupled receptors; HDAC: Histone deacetylase; ILC2s: Type 2 innate lymphoid cells; IL-6: Interleukin-6; IL-23: Interleukin-23; NF-κB: Nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells; SCFA: Short-chain fatty acid; SIP: Sphingosine-1-phosphate; Th17: T helper 17 cell; TNF-α: Tumor necrosis factor alpha; Treg: Regulatory T cell. Created with Biorender.com.Fig 2

The gut–lung axis serves as a critical interorgan communication pathway that influences immune responses in long COVID. SARS-CoV-2 infection can induce gut dysbiosis, reducing beneficial bacteria like Bacteroides and Faecalibacterium that produce key immunomodulatory metabolites. These metabolites, including short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) and bile acids, are essential for maintaining immune homeostasis: SCFAs promote regulatory T cell differentiation and suppress NF-κB-mediated inflammation,12 while bile acids modulate host immunity and oxidative stress. Their deficiency is linked to heightened pulmonary inflammation and impaired repair.13 Emerging research in long COVID confirms these mechanistic links. Studies indicate that SCFAs, particularly butyrate, are persistently reduced in patients, correlating with gut microbiota alterations and systemic inflammation. Similarly, bile acid metabolism is disrupted, with a marked decrease in beneficial secondary bile acids.14 Furthermore, elevated levels of trimethylamine N-oxide (TMAO) are associated with key long COVID mechanisms, including dysbiosis, coagulation abnormalities, and chronic inflammation.15 Critically, not all persistent microbial metabolite changes are pathogenic. The driving force behind chronic inflammation is likely the sustained loss of beneficial, anti-inflammatory metabolites (e.g., SCFAs, secondary bile acids), which disrupts immune regulation and tissue homeostasis, thereby perpetuating the pathophysiology of long COVID.

Neurological symptoms in long COVID, such as brain fog, anxiety, and sleep disturbances, are partly mediated by microbiome imbalances through the gut–brain axis. Depletion of beneficial bacteria (e.g., Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus) reduces synthesis of neurotransmitters including serotonin, gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), and dopamine, affecting mood and cognition. Meanwhile, increased permeability of the gut barrier ("leaky gut") permits translocation of bacterial lipopolysaccharides (LPS) into systemic circulation.16 LPS triggers Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4)-mediated activation of microglia and astrocytes, leading to neuroinflammation and neuronal dysfunction. Vagus nerve signaling, which connects gut microbial activity to central nervous system processes, may also be disrupted. These mechanisms contribute to cognitive deficits, fatigue, and autonomic dysregulation observed in long COVID.

When it comes to current therapeutic approaches for long COVID based on the microbiome, probiotics (e.g., Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium strains) and prebiotics (e.g., dietary fibers) stand out as promising means to restore microbial balance. These interventions are designed to enhance beneficial microbiota, generate immunomodulatory metabolites such as SCFAs, and strengthen epithelial barrier function. Clinical trials have demonstrated their potential in reducing the severity of respiratory symptoms and improving overall recovery trajectories.17 As for emerging strategies, fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) is currently under investigation as a treatment for severe, refractory dysbiosis in long COVID, though its application remains in the experimental stage. Additionally, personalized microbiome therapies, which use high-resolution metagenomic profiling and machine learning, offer a tailored approach to treatment. However, these advances must be carefully balanced against ethical considerations and potential risks, including unintended microbial shifts and the transmission of pathogens.18

Collectively, much has been learned about the influence of microbiome on long COVID, while significant challenges remain in microbiome research pertaining to long COVID. Key limitations include inconsistent sampling techniques—such as the use of nasopharyngeal, oropharyngeal, or sputum samples, each with distinct biases affecting microbiome representation—as well as frequently limited sample sizes that reduce statistical power and increase error risks. Furthermore, substantial variability in study designs, including differing diagnostic criteria for long COVID, follow-up durations, and a lack of standardized laboratory and bioinformatic protocols, impedes direct comparison and synthesis of results across studies. To overcome these constraints, future research should emphasize standardization of sampling and analytical methods, alongside the adoption of larger-scale longitudinal cohorts. By establishing unified protocols and improving data transparency through shared repositories, studies can better capture temporal dynamics of microbiome changes and strengthen the evidence base for clinical and therapeutic applications.

Summing up, the respiratory microbiome plays a central role in the pathophysiology of long COVID, particularly through mechanisms involving immune dysregulation, chronic inflammation, and epithelial compromise. While microbiome-modulating therapies offer considerable promise, robust clinical evidence is still evolving. Future research must integrate multidisciplinary approaches—combining microbiology, immunology, and clinical medicine—to develop effective interventions. By deepening our understanding of host–microbe interactions in post-COVID recovery, we can pave the way for innovative strategies that enhance respiratory health and quality of life for patients worldwide.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: This study was supported by the National Key Research & Development Program of China (Nos. 2023YFA1800900, 2018YFC0910502) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 32071465, 31871334, 31671374).

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Bo Wang: Writing – original draft, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation. Yuan Wang: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software. Kang Ning: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
